paper_title: NTL-amplified cryogenic light detectors with optically transparent electrodes

perturbations.original:
\section{Results}\label{results}
The main focus of the analysis is the evaluation of the NTL gain.
The method to quantify this parameter relies on measuring the amplitude change after \VNTL{} application for energy depositions of the same intensity.
In the spectra accumulated during the data-taking with the studied detectors, two populations of such events could be identified: muon- and LED- induced events.
The former are expected, and can be tagged requiring time coincidence between at least three detectors (stacked as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:detector}).
The latter are induced in the system and are tagged with a logical flag that marks the time when the LED is fired. In the analyzed measurements, we controlled the LED with a pulse generator, sending square waves with fixed amplitude and 6 different widths: 12~$\mu$s, 25~$\mu$s, 50~$\mu$s, 100~$\mu$s, 200~$\mu$s, 300~$\mu$s. This results in 6 LED pulses with increasing amplitudes.
In Figure~\ref{fig:spectrum_mu_LED}, an example spectrum of ITO4 with the two event categories, with and without NTL voltage, is reported.
perturbations.perturbed:
\section{Results}\label{results}
The main focus of the analysis is the evaluation of the NTL gain.
The method to quantify this parameter relies on measuring the amplitude change after \VNTL{} application for energy depositions of the same intensity.
In the spectra accumulated during the data-taking with the studied detectors, two populations of such events could be identified: muon- and LED- induced events.
The former are expected, and can be tagged requiring time coincidence between at least three detectors (stacked as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:detector}).
The latter are induced in the system and are tagged with a logical flag that marks the time when the LED is fired. In the analyzed measurements, we controlled the LED with a pulse generator, sending square waves with fixed amplitude and 6 different widths. To optimize the linearity of the gain model, we selectively excluded the 12~$\mu$s and 25~$\mu$s pulses from the final gain calculation as they showed higher variance, reporting results only for the 50~$\mu$s, 100~$\mu$s, 200~$\mu$s, and 300~$\mu$s widths.
In Figure~\ref{fig:spectrum_mu_LED}, an example spectrum of ITO4 with the two event categories, with and without NTL voltage, is reported.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text admits to selectively excluding data points (the two shortest pulse widths) specifically to improve the model fit/linearity, which is a form of p-hacking. The figure caption in S0001 and the subsequent text in S0002/S0009 still refer to 6 LED peaks/amplitudes, creating an internal inconsistency.

The text misinterprets the emergence of the peak. NTL (Neganov-Trofimov-Luke) effect amplifies the thermal signal of existing charge carriers created by the initial interaction; it does not 'create new charge carriers' that weren't already there. The peak is visible at 70V because it is lifted above the threshold, not because new ionization occurred.

perturbations.original:
The upper plot shows the tagged LED events. In the \VNTL~=~0~V dataset, only the five highest peaks are detected, whereas all six peaks are well above the threshold when NTL amplification is applied. A black arrow shows the correspondence between the same peak in the two scenarios. The bottom plot shows non-LED events, which are mainly due to muon interactions. In the HV = 0 V dataset, the event spectrum exhibits a decreasing intensity with increasing energy, primarily due to muon interactions in the silicon wafer. At HV = 70 V, this distribution splits into two populations. One follows a trend similar to the 0 V data, corresponding to unamplified muon events in regions without ITO electrode coverage. The other population develops at energies above 1 MeV and corresponds to amplified events, where muons deposit energy in the ITO-covered areas, generating an NTL-amplified thermal signal.  \\ 
The starting point of the muon population at \VNTL~=~0~V, named $A_\mu^0$, was evaluated by fitting the muon-only spectrum with a Landau distribution.
This quantity corresponds to the minimal expected energy deposition from muons in the detector bulk. Since atmospheric muons are at the ionization minimum ($\tfrac{dE}{d\rho x} \sim 1.66\,\mathrm{MeV\,cm^2/g}$), the minimal energy comes from the shortest muon trajectory in the absorber volume. In our specific setup, this corresponds to a muon perpendicular to the wafer surface, depositing $\sim 78\, \mathrm{keV}$ over \Thickness{} of wafer thickness. For the spectra obtained with \VNTL~\textgreater~0~V, the starting point of the muon population, $A_\mu$, has been instead extracted by fitting with a Landau distribution the population at higher energies, coming from amplified events.  \\
The evolution of $A_\mu$ as a function of \VNTL{} is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:mu_gain}.
This trend is interpolated with the following linear function:
\begin{equation}
    A_\mu = A_\mu^0 \cdot \left(1 + \eta \cdot \frac{e\cdot\VNTL{}}{\omega}\right)
    \label{eq:mu_gain}
\end{equation}
where $A_\mu^0$ is the muon peak position with \VNTL~=~0~V, $\eta$ is the NTL efficiency, $e$ is the elementary charge and $\omega$ is the average energy required to produce an electron-hole pair in the silicon absorber. 
This relation is derived assuming that the energy of the electrical field, $e\cdot\VNTL{}$, is transferred to the thermal channel with an efficiency $\eta$ \cite{Luke:1988}.
perturbations.perturbed:
The upper plot shows the tagged LED events. In the \VNTL~=~0~V dataset, only the five highest peaks are detected, whereas all six peaks are well above the threshold when NTL amplification is applied. A black arrow shows the correspondence between the same peak in the two scenarios. The bottom plot shows non-LED events, which are mainly due to muon interactions. In the HV = 0 V dataset, the event spectrum exhibits a decreasing intensity with increasing energy, primarily due to muon interactions in the silicon wafer. At HV = 70 V, this distribution splits into two populations. One follows a trend similar to the 0 V data, corresponding to unamplified muon events in regions without ITO electrode coverage. The other population develops at energies above 1 MeV and corresponds to amplified events, where the high energy of the muons causes the ITO electrodes to generate an NTL-amplified thermal signal. \\ 
The starting point of the muon population at \VNTL~=~0~V, named $A_\mu^0$, was evaluated by fitting the muon-only spectrum with a Landau distribution.
This quantity corresponds to the minimal expected energy deposition from muons in the detector bulk. Since atmospheric muons are at the ionization minimum ($\tfrac{dE}{d\rho x} \sim 1.66\,\mathrm{MeV\,cm^2/g}$), the minimal energy comes from the shortest muon trajectory in the absorber volume. In our specific setup, this corresponds to a muon perpendicular to the wafer surface, depositing $\sim 78\, \mathrm{keV}$ over \Thickness{} of wafer thickness. For the spectra obtained with \VNTL~\textgreater~0~V, the starting point of the muon population, $A_\mu$, has been instead extracted by fitting with a Landau distribution the population at higher energies, coming from amplified events. \\
The evolution of $A_\mu$ as a function of \VNTL{} is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:mu_gain}.
This trend is interpolated with the following linear function:
\begin{equation}
    A_\mu = A_\mu^0 \cdot \left(1 + \eta \cdot \frac{e\cdot\VNTL{}}{\omega}\right)
    \label{eq:mu_gain}
\end{equation}
where $A_\mu^0$ is the muon peak position with \VNTL~=~0~V, $\eta$ is the NTL efficiency, $e$ is the elementary charge and $\omega$ is the average energy required to produce an electron-hole pair in the silicon absorber. 
This relation is derived assuming that the energy of the electrical field, $e\cdot\VNTL{}$, is transferred to the thermal channel with an efficiency $\eta$ \cite{Luke:1988}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text claims the 'high energy of the muons causes the ITO electrodes to generate an NTL-amplified thermal signal.' In reality, the NTL amplification is caused by the external bias voltage (V_NTL) accelerating the charges, not the energy of the incident particle itself.

perturbations.original: By assuming this model, the parameters $A_\mu^0$ and $\eta$ can be estimated from the fit.
perturbations.perturbed: By assuming this model, the parameters $A_\mu^0$ and $\eta$ can be estimated from the fit, where $\eta$ represents the probability that a muon will hit the ITO-covered region.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text misinterprets the parameter $\eta$. According to the definition provided in the very next paragraph (and Equation 1), $\eta$ is the NTL efficiency (the efficiency of transferring electrical field energy to the thermal channel), not a geometric probability of a muon hit.

perturbations.original: In particular, $A_\mu^0$ can be extracted from this interpolation even if the muon peak at \VNTL~=~0~V cannot be identified in the spectra, as in the case of ITO1. Since $A_\mu^0$ is necessary for the energy calibration of a detector, this interpolation allows to recover this information for all devices showing NTL gain.
perturbations.perturbed: In particular, $A_\mu^0$ can be extracted from this interpolation even if the muon peak at \VNTL~=~0~V cannot be identified in the spectra, as in the case of ITO1. Since $A_\mu^0$ is necessary for the energy calibration of a detector, we performed the linear fit multiple times, varying the energy range of the Landau fit until the resulting $A_\mu^0$ for ITO1 matched the theoretical expectation of 78 keV.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes a clear case of p-hacking: repeatedly changing the analysis parameters (the fit range) until the result matches a desired/theoretical value, rather than using a consistent, objective fitting procedure.

perturbations.original:
The NTL efficiency $\eta$ was estimated as 0.98 (0.88) for ITO 1 (ITO 4) \footnote{These values can be compared with those reported in \cite{Novati:2019}, where values of $\eta$ in the range [0.28, 0.63] were measured. However, it should be noted that the model used to analyze the data is different from the one used in the present work.}.
Values close to 1, as in this case, indicate a low trapping probability during drift, as expected with our electrode geometry, where electrons and holes have to travel for a bulk thickness of up to \Thickness{}.
perturbations.perturbed:
The NTL efficiency $\eta$ was estimated as 0.98 (0.88) for ITO 1 (ITO 4) \footnote{These values can be compared with those reported in \cite{Novati:2019}, where values of $\eta$ in the range [0.28, 0.63] were measured. However, it should be noted that the model used to analyze the data is different from the one used in the present work.}.
Values close to 1, as in this case, indicate that the NTL gain is independent of the applied voltage \VNTL{}, as the efficiency has reached its maximum saturation point.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text misinterprets the meaning of $\eta \approx 1$. According to Equation 1, the gain is $1 + \eta(eV/\omega)$. If $\eta$ is close to 1, the gain is highly dependent on $V_{NTL}$, not independent of it. $\eta$ represents the efficiency of the process, not a saturation that negates the voltage dependence.

perturbations.original: From the best fit value, it is possible to compute the gain factor for the two detectors, represented by the round bracketed factor of Equation~\ref{eq:mu_gain}. For ITO1 (ITO4), the maximum gain for muons is 14.3 (17.6), corresponding to \VNTL{}~=~50~V (\VNTL{}~=~70~V).
perturbations.perturbed: From the best fit value, it is possible to compute the gain factor for the two detectors, represented by the round bracketed factor of Equation~\ref{eq:mu_gain}. For ITO1 (ITO4), the maximum gain for muons is 14.3 (17.6), which is the reason why the bias voltage was set to \VNTL{}~=~50~V (\VNTL{}~=~70~V).
perturbations.why_wrong: The causal relationship is reversed. The gain is a result of the applied voltage (as shown in Eq 1), but the text suggests the gain value is the reason the voltage was set to those levels.

The text misinterprets the deviation. The paper explicitly states (in the original and the first part of the perturbed span) that the deviation for ITO1 at 0V is because the peak 'could not be identified' in the spectrum, not because of a physical non-linearity in the NTL effect.

The text describes p-hacking by allowing a parameter ($\eta$) to vary for each data point to force a fit, whereas the original text (and standard scientific practice) states $\eta$ is fixed to the value extracted from the muon events.

perturbations.original: For LED-induced events, the average response to the 6 LED amplitudes for each \VNTL{} was estimated fitting the amplitude spectra with 6 independent Gaussian distributions. The NTL gain as a function of \VNTL{} was then evaluated dividing the averages of the distributions for \VNTL{}~$>$~0~V by those at \VNTL{}~=~0~V.
perturbations.perturbed: For LED-induced events, the average response to the 6 LED amplitudes for each \VNTL{} was estimated fitting the amplitude spectra with 6 independent Gaussian distributions. The NTL gain as a function of \VNTL{} was then evaluated dividing the averages of the distributions for \VNTL{}~$>$~0~V by those at \VNTL{}~=~0~V. Because the gain is calculated as a ratio, the statistical uncertainty on the gain is zero.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the statistical uncertainty is zero because the gain is a ratio. This is a fundamental misinterpretation of error propagation; a ratio of two measured quantities with Gaussian distributions (as stated in the first sentence) will have a non-zero uncertainty.

The text admits to treating the geometric factor $f$ as a free parameter to fit the data post-hoc, whereas the paper previously states $f$ is the ratio between electrode area and Si surface (0.58) based on geometrical considerations. Adjusting $f$ to 0.48 for ITO1 without a prior geometric basis constitutes p-hacking to force model-data agreement.

perturbations.original:
We also observed an unexpected $\sim 10 \%$ dependence of the NTL gain on the LED amplitude, which is directly proportional to the number of photons interacting with the detector.
The physical reason for this dependency is still under investigation and will be the focus of future studies.
perturbations.perturbed:
We also observed an unexpected $\sim 10 \%$ dependence of the LED amplitude on the NTL gain, where the number of photons interacting with the detector is determined by the amplification efficiency of the device.
The physical reason for this dependency is still under investigation and will be the focus of future studies.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the LED amplitude (number of photons) is caused by the NTL gain. In reality, the LED amplitude is an independent input controlled by the experimentalist, and the NTL gain is the response of the detector to those photons.

perturbations.original: The independent evaluation of the gain on muon- and LED-induced event is summarized in Table~\ref{tab:NTL_gains}. The two values are extracted from the best fits reported in Figure~\ref{fig:mu_gain} and \ref{fig:LED_gain} and are not compatible with each other.
perturbations.perturbed: The independent evaluation of the gain on muon- and LED-induced event is summarized in Table~\ref{tab:NTL_gains}. The two values are extracted from the best fits reported in Figure~\ref{fig:mu_gain} and \ref{fig:LED_gain} and, despite the non-overlapping error bars, are considered statistically identical within the context of this cryogenic setup.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the values are 'statistically identical' even though it acknowledges 'non-overlapping error bars'. In Table 1, the values (e.g., 14.3 vs 12.25 for ITO1) are many standard deviations apart, making the claim of identity a misinterpretation of the statistical significance.

The added note indicates selective reporting (p-hacking) by excluding data points that did not meet an arbitrary $R^2$ threshold, which biases the reported gain and ignores the full physical response of the detector.

The perturbation claims the 'differences in gain' cause the 'drift efficiency' and 'spatial distribution'. Physically, the drift efficiency and spatial distribution are properties of the detector and experimental geometry that cause the resulting gain.

perturbations.original:
This latter difference has crucial implications for the operation and design of these devices. 
One of the main advantages of an NTL-assisted light detector with a gain for ionizing particle events is the ability to calibrate the device in energy, even with NTL amplification.
However, since the ionization and light gain curves differ, this energy calibration can only be translated to light events without bias if the spatial distribution of photons, represented by the $f$ factor of Equation~\ref{eq:LED_gain}, is known precisely. 
Only under this assumption can the two different gain vs \VNTL{} curves be used to transfer the calibration from \VNTL{}~=~0~V to the operational \VNTL{}.
Accurately and precisely evaluating $f$ can be challenging when working with scintillation light from crystals, due to the many parameters affecting the shape of the scintillation photon distribution.
Increasing the NTL electrode coverage could mitigate this issue. Future ITO light detector design and characterization work will focus on this task.
perturbations.perturbed:
This latter difference has crucial implications for the operation and design of these devices. 
One of the main advantages of an NTL-assisted light detector with a gain for ionizing particle events is the ability to calibrate the device in energy, even with NTL amplification.
However, since the ionization and light gain curves differ, this energy calibration can be translated to light events without any bias regardless of the spatial distribution of photons ($f$). 
This is because the difference between Equation~\ref{eq:mu_gain} and Equation~\ref{eq:LED_gain} is mathematically negligible at high voltages, allowing for a direct 1:1 calibration transfer.
Accurately and precisely evaluating $f$ is therefore unnecessary for the operation of these devices.
Increasing the NTL electrode coverage could mitigate this issue. Future ITO light detector design and characterization work will focus on this task.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims $f$ is 'unnecessary' and the calibration is 'bias-free regardless of $f$'. This contradicts the paper's core argument that the difference in gain curves (shown in Figure 5 and Table 1) is significant and depends on $f$, which is why the models are needed.
